anchor.snippet: Velocity is a measurement of speed in a certain direction of motion
The scalar absolute value ( magnitude ) of velocity is called speed
If there is a change in speed, direction or both, then the object is said to be undergoing an acceleration
The average velocity of an object over a period of time is its change in position
The instantaneous velocity of an object is the limit average velocity as the time interval approaches zero

annotations.label: Area under v–t graph is displacement

anchor.snippet: the area under a velocity vs. time ( v vs. t graph) is the displacement
Speed, the scalar magnitude of a velocity vector, denotes only how fast an object is moving
To have a constant velocity , an object must have a constant speed in a constant direction
a car moving at a constant 20 kilometres per hour in a circular path has a constant speed
velocity is measured in metres per second

annotations.label: Velocity as rate of change of position

anchor.snippet: Velocity is defined as the rate of change of position with respect to time
The average velocity is always less than or equal to the average speed of an object

annotations.label: Velocity as slope of tangent line

anchor.snippet: the instantaneous velocity (or, simply, velocity) can be thought of as the slope of the tangent line to the curve at any point

annotations.label: Average speed over equal time intervals

anchor.snippet: When a particle moves with different uniform speeds
average speed is given by the arithmetic mean of the speeds
When a particle moves different distances
average speed is given by the harmonic mean of the speeds
instantaneous acceleration is defined as the derivative of velocity with respect to time

annotations.label: Velocity as area under a–t graph

anchor.snippet: velocity is expressed as the area under an a ( t ) acceleration vs. time graph
In the special case of constant acceleration, velocity can be studied using the suvat equations
it is possible to relate the displacement and the average velocity
known as the Torricelli equation
Newton's second law defines momentum , p, as a vector that is the product of an object's mass and velocity
The kinetic energy of a moving object is dependent on its velocity
drag is a force acting opposite to the relative motion of any object moving with respect to a surrounding fluid
is dependent on the square of velocity and is given as
Escape velocity is the minimum speed a ballistic object needs to escape from a massive body such as Earth

anchor.section: The Lorentz factor of special relativity
anchor.snippet: the dimensionless Lorentz factor appears frequently, and is given by
Relative velocity is a measurement of velocity between two objects as determined in a single coordinate system
the velocity of object A relative to object B is defined as the difference of the two velocity vectors
the velocities are scalars and the equation is either
velocity is broken up into components that correspond with each dimensional axis
The magnitude of this vector represents speed and is found by the distance formula
In three-dimensional systems where there is an additional z-axis, the corresponding velocity component is defined as
a two-dimensional velocity is described by a radial velocity
Both arise from angular velocity , which is the rate of rotation about the origin
The transverse velocity is the component of velocity along a circle centered at the origin
The radial speed (or magnitude of the radial velocity) is the dot product of the velocity vector and the unit vector in the radial direction
The transverse speed (or magnitude of the transverse velocity) is the magnitude of the cross product of the unit vector in the radial direction and the velocity vector
Angular momentum in scalar form is the mass times the distance to the origin times the transverse velocity
is known as moment of inertia
These relations are known as Kepler's laws of planetary motion
